---
title: "Using Randomization & Stratification to Overcome A Common Cause Confounder"
subtitle: "The Paradox of Who Really Is the Better Surgeon"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: cosmo
    echo: false
    warning: false
    message: false
bibliography: references.bib
---
```{python}
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

# Color palette — defined once and reused throughout
dreamy_color = '#4E79A7'
duck_color   = '#E15759'

# Load both datasets
patients_df            = pd.read_csv('patients_data.csv')
patients_randomized_df = pd.read_csv('patients_data_randomized.csv')

# Split observational data by doctor
dreamy_obs = patients_df[patients_df['doctor_name'] == 'Doc Dreamy']
duck_obs   = patients_df[patients_df['doctor_name'] == 'Doc Duck']

# Split randomized data by doctor
dreamy_rand = patients_randomized_df[patients_randomized_df['doctor_name'] == 'Doc Dreamy']
duck_rand   = patients_randomized_df[patients_randomized_df['doctor_name'] == 'Doc Duck']

# Compute means
dreamy_obs_mean  = dreamy_obs['post_surgical_score'].mean()
duck_obs_mean    = duck_obs['post_surgical_score'].mean()
dreamy_rand_mean = dreamy_rand['post_surgical_score'].mean()
duck_rand_mean   = duck_rand['post_surgical_score'].mean()
```

## The Paradox: Who Really Is the Better Surgeon?

Imagine you need to choose between two surgeons of similar rank at the same hospital. The first, Doc Dreamy, fits every stereotype of a great doctor — silver-rimmed glasses, Ivy League diplomas on the wall, measured speech. The second, Doc Duck, looks more like a butcher: overweight, large hands, unkempt, no visible credentials.

Counterintuitively, the surgeon who doesn't look the part may actually be the better choice. As @taleb2017 argues, succeeding despite not fitting the expected appearance suggests overcoming significant perceptual bias — a form of skin in the game that filters out incompetence.

So who actually is the better surgeon? The data tells a surprising story.

---

## Observational Data: A Misleading Victory for Doc Dreamy
```{python}
patients_df.head()
```

With 100 patients choosing their own surgeon, Doc Dreamy's average post-surgical symptom score is 2.80 and Doc Duck's is 3.38. Since lower scores mean better outcomes, Doc Dreamy appears to win — and a two-sample t-test confirms the difference is statistically significant (t = −2.317, p = 0.023).
```{python}
#| label: fig-observational
#| fig-cap: "Doc Dreamy's patients show lower symptom scores on average, but this apparent advantage deserves scrutiny. Lower scores are better."

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))

# Scatter points
ax.scatter(
    dreamy_obs['patient'], dreamy_obs['post_surgical_score'],
    color=dreamy_color, marker='o', s=60, alpha=0.8, label='Doc Dreamy', zorder=3
)
ax.scatter(
    duck_obs['patient'], duck_obs['post_surgical_score'],
    color=duck_color, marker='^', s=60, alpha=0.8, label='Doc Duck', zorder=3
)

# Mean lines
ax.axhline(dreamy_obs_mean, color=dreamy_color, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.8, zorder=2)
ax.axhline(duck_obs_mean,   color=duck_color,   linestyle='--', linewidth=1.8, zorder=2)

# Mean annotations
ax.text(
    101, dreamy_obs_mean, f'Mean: {dreamy_obs_mean:.2f} (○)',
    color=dreamy_color, va='center', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold'
)
ax.text(
    101, duck_obs_mean, f'Mean: {duck_obs_mean:.2f} (△)',
    color=duck_color, va='center', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold'
)

ax.set_title('Post-Surgical Symptom Score by Patient and Doctor', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Patient Number', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Post-Surgical Symptom Score\n(Lower is Better)', fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlim(0, 115)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The raw numbers look clear. But raw numbers can deceive. The question we need to ask is whether this association is causal — or whether something else is driving it.

---

## The Hidden Confounder: Patient Severity Explains It All

A common-cause confounder creates associations that have nothing to do with causation. The classic example: puddles on the road and people carrying umbrellas are correlated, but puddles don't cause umbrellas. Rain causes both.

Here, the confounder is patient severity. Both surgeons have full schedules, but Doc Dreamy books three weeks in advance while Doc Duck books one week out. Patients who are not in a rush — typically those with lower severity — choose Doc Dreamy based on his polished profile. Patients who need surgery quickly — typically higher severity — end up with Doc Duck. Higher severity patients naturally have worse outcomes. So Doc Duck looks worse not because he is worse, but because he inherited the harder cases.

Patient severity causes both surgeon choice and surgical outcomes. That makes it a textbook common-cause confounder, and it means the association we saw in the observational data is spurious.

---

## Solution 1: Randomization — Break the Confounding Path

The gold standard fix is a randomized controlled trial. Instead of letting patients choose their surgeon, we randomly assign them. This severs the link between patient severity and surgeon choice. Now both doctors get a similar mix of easy and hard cases, and any difference in outcomes reflects actual surgical skill.
```{python}
patients_randomized_df.head()
```

Under random assignment, Doc Duck's average score is 2.71 and Doc Dreamy's is 3.46. The rankings have flipped completely. A t-test confirms this difference is statistically significant (t = 2.734, p = 0.007). Doc Dreamy is not just not the better surgeon — he is actually the worse one.
```{python}
#| label: fig-randomized
#| fig-cap: "With random assignment, Doc Duck outperforms Doc Dreamy. The confounding relationship is broken and the true causal effect is revealed."

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))

ax.scatter(
    dreamy_rand['patient'], dreamy_rand['post_surgical_score'],
    color=dreamy_color, marker='o', s=60, alpha=0.8, label='Doc Dreamy', zorder=3
)
ax.scatter(
    duck_rand['patient'], duck_rand['post_surgical_score'],
    color=duck_color, marker='^', s=60, alpha=0.8, label='Doc Duck', zorder=3
)

ax.axhline(dreamy_rand_mean, color=dreamy_color, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.8, zorder=2)
ax.axhline(duck_rand_mean,   color=duck_color,   linestyle='--', linewidth=1.8, zorder=2)

ax.text(
    101, dreamy_rand_mean, f'Mean: {dreamy_rand_mean:.2f} (○)',
    color=dreamy_color, va='center', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold'
)
ax.text(
    101, duck_rand_mean, f'Mean: {duck_rand_mean:.2f} (△)',
    color=duck_color, va='center', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold'
)

ax.set_title('Post-Surgical Symptom Score (Randomized Assignment)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Patient Number', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Post-Surgical Symptom Score\n(Lower is Better)', fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlim(0, 115)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Randomization is a powerful tool precisely because it doesn't require us to know all the confounders in advance. By making surgeon assignment independent of everything else, it neutralizes confounders we haven't even thought of.

---

## Solution 2: Stratification — When You Can't Randomize

Most of the time, randomization isn't possible. In those cases, stratification is the next best option. Instead of comparing all of Doc Dreamy's patients to all of Doc Duck's patients, we compare patients of similar severity — apples to apples.

The figure below plots patient severity on the x-axis and post-surgical score on the y-axis. The shaded region highlights severity scores between −1 and 1, where both doctors have substantial overlap in patient mix. This is the zone where a fair comparison is possible.
```{python}
#| label: fig-stratification
#| fig-cap: "Within the highlighted severity range (−1 to 1), Doc Dreamy's blue circles tend to sit higher (worse) than Doc Duck's red triangles — the opposite of what the raw averages suggested."

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))

# Shaded region — draw first so it sits behind the points
shade_xmin = -1
shade_xmax =  1
ax.axvspan(shade_xmin, shade_xmax, alpha=0.15, color='gray', zorder=0)

# Scatter points
ax.scatter(
    dreamy_obs['severity'], dreamy_obs['post_surgical_score'],
    color=dreamy_color, marker='o', s=60, alpha=0.8, label='Doc Dreamy', zorder=3
)
ax.scatter(
    duck_obs['severity'], duck_obs['post_surgical_score'],
    color=duck_color, marker='^', s=60, alpha=0.8, label='Doc Duck', zorder=3
)

# Overall mean lines
ax.axhline(dreamy_obs_mean, color=dreamy_color, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.8, zorder=2)
ax.axhline(duck_obs_mean,   color=duck_color,   linestyle='--', linewidth=1.8, zorder=2)

# Mean annotations — placed on the right
x_max = patients_df['severity'].max()
ax.text(
    x_max + 0.1, dreamy_obs_mean, f'Mean: {dreamy_obs_mean:.2f} (○)',
    color=dreamy_color, va='center', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold'
)
ax.text(
    x_max + 0.1, duck_obs_mean, f'Mean: {duck_obs_mean:.2f} (△)',
    color=duck_color, va='center', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold'
)

ax.set_title('Post-Surgical Symptom Score by Patient Severity', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Initial Patient Severity', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Post-Surgical Symptom Score\n(Lower is Better)', fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlim(patients_df['severity'].min() - 0.2, patients_df['severity'].max() + 1.5)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Within the shaded region, Doc Dreamy's circles sit higher than Doc Duck's triangles — meaning Doc Dreamy actually produces worse outcomes for patients of comparable severity. Outside that region, the comparison breaks down because the two doctors are treating very different patient populations. Doc Duck handles most of the high-severity cases on the right side of the chart; Doc Dreamy handles most of the low-severity cases on the left. That separation is the confounder doing its work.

---

## The Takeaway

The raw data made Doc Dreamy look like the superior surgeon. He wasn't. Patient severity was operating as a puppeteer behind the scenes, routing easier cases to the polished doctor and harder cases to the one who didn't look the part. Once we either randomize or stratify by severity, the picture reverses entirely.

This is the lesson that extends far beyond surgery. Whenever you see a striking association in observational data, the first question to ask is: what common cause might be driving this? Draw the causal graph. Think about what causes what. Then either randomize to break the confounding path, or stratify to control for it directly.

The numbers weren't lying. They were just answering a different question than the one you thought you were asking.

---

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
